text: 54g of fat. saturated fat, omega3, and polyunsaturated fat are doing most of the work. for nuts, fat quality has to justify the calorie density.
20g of carbs. fibre is doing a lot of the work here. for a nut, the score starts with fat chemistry and then asks what else it brings.
17g of protein. bioavailability and amino acid quality are both strong. protein is support here, not the whole argument.
vitamin e at 20% daily value stands out most. that is only mild support overall.
magnesium at 32% daily value, zinc at 18% daily value, iron at 18% daily value stand out most. useful, but still not a major mineral edge.

on the plus side: texture helps them feel satisfying because that can make moderation easier than with softer snack foods. holds up as a better snack default than most packaged options because that real-world swap value counts. fat and texture help satiety because they can hold up better than many snack foods.

on the weaker side: good versions can get expensive because that affects how realistic frequent use is. cost or allergy issues can limit use because practical downsides still count. protein is useful but not elite because nuts help, but they are not protein stars.
C tier. Best only in small amounts when the calories stay under control.
